data.title: Hong Kong’s AI education quest needs human connection to succeed
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
In a recent Chinese-language blog post, Hong Kong’s undersecretary for education Dr Jeff Sze Chun-fai wrote about the city’s digital education blueprint. We at Just Feel welcome the government’s commitment to strengthening the city’s artificial intelligence-related competencies, enhancing teachers’ professional development and expanding resources for schools. But as Hong Kong advances its digital transformation, one principle deserves equal weight: meaningful learning, whether for teachers or students, is fundamentally relational.
Professor Cheng Kai-ming’s long-standing call to “return learning to students” emphasises the need to cultivate self-regulated learners, active learners and a strong sense of student agency. From our frontline work through the Compassionate School Programme, we consistently see that such agency is not developed by technology alone. It grows in emotionally safe and respectful environments where students can identify feelings, express needs and build supportive peer relationships.
This reflects the principle often described as “Maslow before bloom”: when emotional needs are supported, higher-order learning becomes possible.
Three weeks ago, our team joined the Belt and Road Teacher Development Exchange Project in Shanghai, under the theme “Social and Emotional Learning for Teachers and Students in the AI Era”. After two days of largely one-way presentations in which participants remained seated with limited interaction, we led an experiential session that invited them to stand up, move around and engage in structured conversations designed to help them get to know one another, listen with curiosity and build authentic connection. Participants – academics, policymakers and officials from 22 countries – told us they wished such relational grounding had occurred on day one.
Experiencing this sense of psychological safety and human connection met their needs for trust, belonging and clarity – conditions that enabled them to participate more openly and make sense of AI’s implications in a more grounded way. Such relational readiness is often a prerequisite for deep learning, especially when navigating unfamiliar technologies.
For Hong Kong’s digital education blueprint to realise its promise, technological innovation must advance together with human connection. AI can support teachers by taking over routine tasks and expanding learning opportunities, but it cannot replace the relational conditions that make learning possible in the first place.
When teachers are equipped not only with digital competencies but also with social and emotional learning and compassionate communication skills, they are better able to notice students’ emotional cues, foster psychological safety and cultivate supportive peer relationships. This is how digital education becomes more than an upgrade in tools – it becomes a foundation for human growth.
In the age of AI, connection is not a “nice to have”; it is the condition that enables learning itself. That is what it means to build classrooms grounded in “connection before solution”.
Raymond Yang Sze-ngai, co-founder and executive director, Just Feel
International DSE an idea worth exploring – with caution
I refer to the letter, “Create an international DSE to extend Hong Kong’s educational reach” (November 3), which convincingly argues that Hong Kong should adapt its education system to a global context by promoting an internationalised Diploma of Secondary Education.
A primary strength of the proposal is the appeal of local universities to international students. The suggestion to model an International DSE on the International Baccalaureate is exactly what is needed to draw international students and benchmark our curriculum globally.
However, for an International DSE to be truly successful, local interests must be safeguarded. In the development of such a curriculum, the government must actively involve frontline educators, curriculum experts and the Hong Kong Examinations and Assessment Authority from the outset to ensure fairness and quality. What is essential is that the integrity of the local DSE is preserved, ensuring that the creation of an international curriculum in no way disadvantages local students.
If we can establish a globally recognised exam that benefits both international and local educational ecosystems, Hong Kong will solidify its status as a unique bridge between Eastern and Western teaching traditions.
Justin Ip, Sha Tin
Young Chinese need a more realistic view of marriage
It would appear China’s efforts to promote marriage by making it easier for people to register their marriage anywhere in the country are paying off, with the number of marriage registrations in the first three quarters of this year rising by 8.5 per cent year on year.
Besides the incentives and policy tweaks, education should also be a major tool to encourage young people to get married, have children and, most of all, enjoy married life.
Young people should learn to have realistic expectations of marriage. A marriage often starts on a high note, but that can’t last forever. After a while, things might become a little boring or even a bit sour. This is normal.
Public education should also highlight the joys of raising children. People should know that having a little girl or boy would give one at least as much pleasure as raising a dog or cat, and it lasts much longer too.
Roland Guettler, Lai Chi Kok

data.title: Japanese consumers fear curbs on eel delicacy ahead of global wildlife forum
data.description:
At an eel restaurant near Tokyo, four friends sit down to eat a Japanese delicacy now the subject of a heated international debate as its numbers decline.
The eel, hugely popular across Japan, is also endangered and will be discussed at a key global forum that regulates the trade of threatened wildlife, starting in Uzbekistan on Monday.
“It’s a luxury dish that we eat to treat ourselves or to celebrate an occasion,” Yukiko Takahashi, a 52-year-old saleswoman, said as she tucked into her food at Hiranuma Suisan.
“Today, we friends came here to lift our spirits.”
Japan is strongly opposed to a proposal by the European Union, Panama and Honduras to list all 17 species of eels worldwide under the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species (CITES), which restricts trade of protected wildlife.
Up to 85 per cent of global eel consumption occurs in East Asia, particularly in Japan, which imported almost three-quarters of the 61,000 tons consumed there last year.
Eel populations are falling worldwide, scientists say, largely due to factors linked to human activity, such as the pollution of waterways, destruction of wetlands, hydroelectric dams and fishing.

Estimating population sizes remains difficult because of the mysterious life cycle of the snakelike fish.
The Japanese eel, a common species in East Asia, reproduces west of the Mariana Islands, 2,000km to 3,000km (1,240 miles to 1,865 miles) from the Japanese coast.
Its larvae develop into glass eels, or juveniles, as they approach the coast, then live in freshwater for five to 15 years before making the reverse journey.
The Japanese eel, along with the American variety, is listed as endangered on the International Union for Conservation of Nature’s (IUCN) Red List.
The European eel is considered critically endangered.
Eels, called “unagi” in Japan, have been consumed there for millennia and are now typically grilled on skewers and dipped in a soy and rice wine sauce.
People are willing to face ever-increasing prices to continue the culinary tradition, driven by significant year-to-year fluctuations in supply.

“Customers understand that it’s a luxury dish,” chef Tomoyuki Takashino said from behind the stove as he skewered an eel he had just killed and filleted.
Unaju, an eel served over rice in a lacquered box, is priced at 5,250 yen (US$34), more than double what the dish might have cost 15 years ago, according to Takashino.
Nearly all eels consumed in Japan come from aquaculture, which relies entirely on the capture or import of glass eels because they do not reproduce in captivity.
Catches of glass eels in Japan have dropped to less than 10 per cent of their 1960s levels.
“We buy the glass eels, which we raise in these tanks,” said Takayuki Hiranuma, president of a hi-tech aquaculture farm that operates the restaurant.
Behind him, some 80,000 eels are kept in six huge reservoirs in 30 degrees Celsius filtered water.
Their weight will increase a thousandfold over 10 months of farming before they are sold or cooked on site.
If eels are listed under Appendix II of the CITES convention, as will be discussed in Uzbekistan, their trade would be more strictly regulated, requiring export permits from the country of origin.
“This is a point that worries us greatly,” Hiranuma said.

Japan argues that it has implemented fishing quotas and farming permits, and cites a study by Tokyo University of Marine Science and Technology that says the Japanese eel population has tripled across East Asia since 1990.
“And we are cooperating with China, South Korea and Taiwan – territories where the Japanese eel lives – to limit the quantities of glass eels captured and improve stock management,” said Tetsuya Kawashima, an official from the Japanese Fisheries Agency.
A United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization expert advisory panel also said in a report this year that the risk of extinction for Japanese and American eels was “low”.
But for Andrew Kerr of the Sustainable Eel Group, such findings failed to take into account eel trafficking, which he called the “greatest wildlife crime” on the planet.
Due to the fact that eels look alike, “all sorts of games are played pretending it’s one species when it’s another”, he said. “So trafficking can thrive.”
Asian supply has long relied on the smuggling of glass eels from the EU, which banned their export in 2010.
Around 100 tons of glass eels transit each year, according to the European police agency Europol.
But with the dismantling of illegal supply chains, European supply had diminished, and there had been a rise in shipments from the Caribbean, threatening that population, Kerr warned.
“It’s not about their imminent risk of extinction. It’s about how do we create some sort of world order in the trade of eel. It’s a global game,” he said.

data.title: For Chinese invested in Japan, public sentiment is a factor to watch in diplomatic row
data.description:
For Gu Chuan and his wife, relocating from China to Japan appeared to be a logical choice.
Last year, when China’s economy slowed and optimism about job prospects dimmed, the Beijing-based tech worker did not want his family to put all its eggs in one basket.
The couple considered several options. Hong Kong was ruled out as too far from home, and so was the United States because of its slew of disputes with Beijing. They finally decided that Gu’s wife would go to Japan to study for an MBA and he would follow when the time was right.
If opportunity allowed, they would even consider having a child in Japan. His wife, who is expected to graduate in 2028, visited hospitals there and believed Japan provided great care with inexpensive medical insurance, said Gu, who asked to use a pseudonym because of the sensitivity of the matter.


He is still in Beijing and, as tensions between China and Japan reach new heights, the couple are waiting to see how the situation turns out before deciding whether to alter their plans.
“We discussed how [the diplomatic freeze] might affect us. I told her Japan’s prime ministers in the past often held short terms, so we’ll wait and see how policies change,” Gu said.
His wife, who is now in Osaka, has not found her daily life affected by the increasing China-Japan tensions, according to Gu. As a Chinese citizen living in Japan, his wife asked not to be identified for this story.
Beijing has reacted with a string of retaliatory measures after Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said that an armed attack against Taiwan could be seen as a potential “survival-threatening situation” that would allow Japan to deploy its military forces.
Her remarks two weeks ago were the most explicit by a sitting Japanese prime minister on how Tokyo might respond to a Taiwan contingency and marked a departure from Japan’s strategic ambiguity on Taiwan – a position also maintained by the United States.
Tokyo later said Takaichi’s remarks were “hypothetical”, and that Japan’s position regarding the self-ruled island had not changed from that stated in the Japan-China Joint Communique of 1972.
But Beijing, which views Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary, has condemned Takaichi’s comments as meddling in China’s internal affairs. Most countries, including the US and Japan, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
China-Japan relations, already troubled in recent years, have plummeted to a new low since Takaichi’s refusal to retract her comments despite protests lodged by Beijing. Senior-level diplomatic talks in Beijing on Tuesday also failed to ease the tensions.
At this early stage, the row is only between diplomatic channels, and China launched restrained counter-attacks in softer areas, such as tourism and culture
Wang Guangtao, Fudan University
China has suspended Japanese seafood imports and intergovernmental meetings, cancelled Japanese films and concerts, advised citizens not to travel to Japan and urged Chinese to reconsider plans to study there.
State media outlets have also intensified their criticisms and warnings, with one commentary affiliated with state broadcaster CCTV even using vulgar language to attack those who shared Takaichi’s views.
Chinese social media users have followed suit, with comments including a call for “a strike to the head” against Japan and praise for China’s diplomats for “showing guts”.
But people on the ground, such as Gu, say they have not seen much protest activity taking place and their daily lives have not been affected. It is a sharp contrast with more than 10 years ago, when thousands of Chinese took to the streets to demonstrate against Japan.
Many Chinese interviewed by the South China Morning Post hoped for matters to quiet down. Several Chinese in Japan reached by the Post said their lives were still “peaceful”, and they were shopping and attending social gatherings as usual. One doctor who gave her name as Joey said she had not even been aware of the recent tensions between the two neighbours.
They said politics did not often play a big part in their lives, especially in choosing where to study and live.
Joey went to Japan to study medicine for practical reasons and stayed on. “The tuition was cheap, it’s close to home and the culture was similar to ours,” she said.
Meanwhile, a survey of Chinese citizens at home found they had not been riled up by the days of fiery language directed at Japan by Beijing.
Xiao Xiaoran, a Beijing-based translator, said she hoped the situation could be contained. She criticised remarks by state media outlets and officials that she said had taken a harsh tone.
“The viewpoints are for the public to see, we are not watching a village brawl. This is a political dispute, ordinary people should not be affected too much by it, and I hope the government doesn’t fan such sentiments for the public to be anti-Japan,” she said, recalling the events of 2012.
The violent events of 2012 unfolded after Tokyo nationalised the Diaoyu Islands, which Japan calls the Senkaku Islands, after buying them from private owners. The group of eight uninhabited islands in the East China Sea has been the subject of a long-running dispute between China and Japan.
Their purchase by Japan led to massive demonstrations in several Chinese cities, including Beijing, that were at first tacitly permitted by the authorities.
Crowds then swamped the streets, smashing Japanese cars and shops. In one extreme case, a protester in central China’s Xian, Shaanxi province, hit a car owner, a Chinese citizen, with a bike lock, causing head injuries. Ultimately, the perpetrator was sentenced to 10 years in prison for the crime.
“The government should cool things down [now] instead of fanning the fire,” Xiao said. “Taking offence too easily is not a good thing.”


She said she would still like to see Japanese films and travel to Japan if getting a visa did not become more difficult.
Wang Guangtao, deputy director of the Centre for Japanese Studies at Fudan University in Shanghai, said public sentiment in the current China-Japan spat had not yet reached the heights of 2012.
“At this early stage, the row is only between diplomatic channels, and China launched restrained counter-attacks in softer areas, such as tourism and culture,” he said, adding that people’s mindsets had changed since then, and they knew what actions would constitute crossing a line.
Wang said indicators of public sentiment, including how many tickets to Japan were cancelled, had to be monitored over a longer period, but he did not believe there would be a large-scale public reaction fuelled by nationalist actions, including protests.
Gu feels “young people nowadays aren’t too concerned with such sentiment. The economy is bad, everybody’s busy with other things”.

data.title: Princess Diana admits adultery in blow to monarchy in 1995 – from the SCMP archive
data.description:
This article was first published on November 22, 1995
By David Wallen and other agencies
New blow to monarchy as Diana admits adultery
Princess Diana’s frank admission of adultery, her graphic description of a three-year battle with bulimia, and her certainty she will never be queen left Britons reeling and dealt a new blow to the monarchy.
In an unprecedented hour-long interview watched by 20 million people, including Hong Kong subscribers to Cable TV, Diana spoke candidly about her failed marriage to Prince Charles, portrayed the royal household as callous, and suggested the heir to the throne was not suited to become king.
Speaking with quiet dignity, Diana said she had been labelled mentally unstable by the royal family as she battled postnatal depression and bulimia, an eating disorder, within two years of her fairy-tale marriage to Charles in 1981.
“I’d like to be a queen of people’s hearts ... but I don’t see myself being queen of this country,” she said.

Buckingham Palace issued a statement after the interview offering to hold discussions with the princess to “help her define her future role in the royal family”, but it refused to comment on what it said were the private lives of its members.
Britons were divided over whether the once-shy princess, who vowed she would “not go quietly”, had won the sympathy vote by baring her soul in such a sensational manner.
Many said her frankness had badly wounded the reputation of Charles and the royal family and slammed the door on any chance of Diana being accepted back into the royal circle.
The interview on the BBC’s Panorama programme led to suggestions from those close to the prince, like former equerry and now a junior defence minister Nicholas Soames that she was “paranoid” and suffering “mental illness”.
But the interview left the impression the princess was strong and lucid.

Andrew Morton, author of a 1991 book which first revealed the depths of Diana’s despair over her marriage, said he believed the interview could work against her.
“I think she overplayed the martyr card. The strategy she sees for herself is not as Princess of Wales but as a princess for the world, someone who will be an ambassadress,” he said.
“That does not fit very well with the fact that today she is now in purdah as far as the palace is concerned.”
Diana, 34, portrayed herself as the wronged woman who “desperately loved” Charles but was given no help to adapt to her role in the media spotlight.

She said she was soon aware that Charles, fed up with a young wife who was not living up to his lofty expectations, had rekindled his affair with Camilla Parker Bowles.
“There were three of us in this marriage, so it was a bit crowded,” she said.
The couple separated in 1992. Diana said she would not stand in the way of divorce from Charles but added “it is not my wish”, for the sake of her two sons.
In one of the most surprising parts of an interview packed with bombshells, Diana admitted having an affair with cavalry officer James Hewitt in 1989. “Yes, I adored him. Yes, I was in love with him.”
Major Hewitt helped write a book 13 months ago describing their affair in detail. In a solo television interview 18 months ago, Charles admitted adultery with Mrs Parker Bowles.

Diana accused advisers and friends of Charles of waging a campaign to discredit her after their separation, referring to them as “the enemy”.
And she claimed the royal household had failed to support her through several years of bulimia.
“It gave everyone a wonderful new label. Diana’s unstable and Diana’s mentally unbalanced,” she said.
A palace statement said: “On the Princess of Wales’ public duties, we will, of course, be talking again to the princess to see how we can help her define her future role and continue to support her as a member of the royal family.”
The palace gave no details of what might be discussed at such talks.
“We are not going to enter into the public debate generated by the Princess of Wales’ interview, either on or off the record, beyond this guidance today.”

data.title: As China-Japan row escalates, South Korea fears being dragged into ‘unwanted flare-up’
data.description:
South Korea is watching with mounting alarm as tensions between China and Japan escalate over the Taiwan Strait, a development that threatens to test Seoul’s pragmatic diplomacy and its balancing act between Beijing and Washington, observers warn.
The dispute is unfolding just as Seoul has worked to stabilise long-strained relations with both regional powers, raising fears that renewed rivalry could force South Korea into unwelcome geopolitical crossfire, they add.
Analysts say the flare-up is particularly troubling because it comes at a moment when the United States is pressing Seoul to play a greater supporting role in any Taiwan-related contingency, including projecting the power of the 28,500 US troops stationed in South Korea beyond the Korean peninsula.
This pressure could complicate Seoul’s long-standing strategy of avoiding overt alignment on Taiwan while maintaining cooperation with both Beijing and Washington.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.

In a related setback, Beijing notified Seoul that it had “temporarily” postponed a planned annual meeting of culture ministers from South Korea, China and Japan set for next week, South Korea’s culture ministry said on Thursday.
The postponement comes amid escalating diplomatic and trade friction between Beijing and Tokyo, triggered by Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s recent suggestion that Japan could intervene militarily if mainland China attacked Taiwan.
China escalated retaliation on Wednesday by suspending imports of Japanese seafood after earlier warning its citizens against travel to Japan. Tokyo, in turn, cautioned Japanese nationals in China to avoid large crowds. Senior diplomats met in Beijing on Tuesday but failed to ease tensions.
Takaichi on Friday said she wanted “constructive” ties with Beijing and Japan’s stance on Taiwan remained “unchanged”.
“At the end of last month, President Xi [Jinping] and I confirmed the broad direction of comprehensively advancing our strategic, mutually beneficial relationship and building a constructive and stable relationship,” she told reporters.

Echoes of 2017
Beijing’s harsh rhetoric and economic measures against Japan mirror its retaliatory actions against South Korea following Seoul’s 2017 deployment of the US THAAD missile defence system, a move that triggered travel bans, cultural restrictions and informal trade barriers, analysts say.
The renewed dispute undermines Seoul’s efforts to restore trilateral cooperation among Northeast Asia’s three major players at a time of rising threats from nuclear-armed North Korea.
“Koreans are watching the dispute with growing concerns as it could drag South Korea into an unwanted flare-up,” warned Lee Wang-hwi, a political-science professor at Ajou University.
He added that “diplomacy is required more than ever” to find a face-saving way for Tokyo and Beijing to de-escalate, and Seoul could serve as mediator because it stood to suffer first from any spillover conflict.
Lee told This Week in Asia that China’s past economic retaliation against Seoul over THAAD “backfired” by failing to block the deployment while fuelling anti-China sentiment, particularly among younger Koreans.
According to the US-based Pew Research Centre, negative views of China in South Korea surged from 31 per cent in 2002 to 80 per cent in 2022.


Some observers say Seoul could see short-term benefits from the dispute, such as an influx of Chinese tourists diverting from Japan. But the long-term outlook is far less favourable.
“The Sino-Japan row would serve no purpose for the three countries whose cooperation is essential for stability and prosperity in Northeast Asia,” said Kim Tae-hyun, a political-science professor at Chung Ang University.
Seoul could raise its diplomatic profile by offering the two countries a pretext to step back “for the sake of regional peace and stability”, he added.
Others see China’s punitive focus on Japan as a strategic warning. Chang Yong-seok, a senior researcher at Seoul National University’s Institute for Peace and Unification Studies, said Beijing selected Japan for “exemplary punishment” to signal consequences for crossing what it considers a red line: Taiwan.
“This is an indirect warning to South Korea and other countries, and we cannot be at ease in the face of mounting tensions,” Chang said. Rising tensions, he added, left Seoul with less diplomatic room as it walked a tightrope between the US and China.
The row comes as South Korea and China have been cautiously mending ties after years of chill, marked by Xi’s visit to the country – his first in 11 years – for a summit with South Korean President Lee Jae-myung earlier this month.

In an unusual diplomatic gesture afterwards, China appeared to take Seoul’s side in its long-running territorial dispute with Japan over Dokdo or the Takeshima Islands, departing from its past practice of avoiding explicit comment.
Chinese Foreign Ministry spokeswoman Mao Ning said on Monday: “We urge Japan to repent for its history of aggression, stick to the path of peaceful development and take concrete actions to earn the trust of its neighbours.”
Meanwhile, the South Korean Navy has reportedly postponed the planned resumption of a joint search-and-rescue drill with Japan’s Maritime Self-Defence Force, originally set for later this month.
The exercise, conducted from 1999 to 2017, had been suspended after a 2018 military confrontation involving a Japanese patrol aircraft.
This year’s planned resumption – meant as a symbolic step towards restoring defence cooperation – was halted after Japan refused mid-air refuelling support for South Korea’s Air Force aerobatic team en route to an air show in Dubai, citing its flight path over Dokdo.
Despite signs of rapprochement, risks remain. Lee of Ajou University warned that South Korea’s plan to build nuclear-powered submarines, along with China’s construction of large steel structures in contested waters of the Yellow Sea, could reignite friction.
“As China wants other countries to respect its core interests, South Korea also wants it to do likewise – don’t do things that could stoke anti-China sentiments as during the THAAD crisis, and rein in North Korea’s nuclear ambition,” he said.

Kang Jun-young, a China expert at Hankuk University of Foreign Studies, said Beijing was likely to continue overtures to Seoul while confronting Tokyo.
“It remains unclear how the China-Japan dispute will affect South Korea, but the Korean government needs to tread carefully to avoid giving the impression that it is taking sides or intervening,” he said.
In an editorial on Wednesday, the JoongAng Daily urged Seoul and Beijing to activate the high-level strategic communication channel agreed at their recent summit to manage escalating tensions and prevent further deterioration in Northeast Asia.
It stressed that South Korea’s nuclear-powered submarines are designed to deter North Korea and that its plans to reprocess spent nuclear fuel are for peaceful purposes.
“Seoul must clearly explain to China that these actions are not targeting China,” the paper said, adding that Seoul must continue pressing Beijing to use its influence over North Korea’s nuclear and missile threats.
Additional reporting by Agence France-Presse

data.title: Marjorie Taylor Greene, ex-Trump loyalist, says to resign from Congress
data.description:
Republican Representative Marjorie Taylor Greene of Georgia, a once-loyal supporter of US President Donald Trump who later became a critic, has said she is resigning from Congress in January.
Greene, in a more than 10-minute video posted online, explained her decision on Friday and said she was “always been despised in Washington, D.C., and just never fit in”.
Greene’s resignation followed a public fallout with Trump in recent months, as the congresswoman criticised him for his stance on files related to Jeffrey Epstein, along with foreign policy and healthcare.
Trump branded her a “traitor” and “wacky”, and said he would endorse a challenger against her when she ran for re-election next year.
She said her last day would be January 5, 2026.
The White House did not immediately respond to a message seeking comment on Friday night.
Greene was one of the most vocal and visible supporters of Trump’s Make America Great Again politics, and she embraced some of his unapologetic political style.
Her break with him was a notable fissure in his grip over conservatives, particularly his most ardent base. But her decision to step down in the face of his opposition put her on the same track as many of the more moderate establishment Republicans before her who went crosswise with Trump.

Greene had been closely tied to the Republican president since she launched her political career five years ago.
In her video on Friday, she underscored her long-time loyalty to Trump except on a few issues and said it was “unfair and wrong” that he attacked her for disagreeing.
“Loyalty should be a two-way street and we should be able to vote our conscience and represent our district’s interest, because our job title is literally ‘representative’,” she said.
Greene swept to office at the forefront of Trump’s Maga movement and swiftly became a lightning rod on Capitol Hill for her often beyond mainstream views.
As she embraced the QAnon conspiracy theory and appeared with white supremacists, Greene was opposed by party leaders but welcomed by Trump. He called her “a real WINNER!”
Yet over time she proved a deft legislator, having aligned herself with then-Republican Party leader Kevin McCarthy, who would go on to become House speaker. She was a trusted voice on the right flank, until McCarthy was ousted in 2023.
While there has been an onslaught of lawmakers from both parties heading for the exits ahead of next fall’s midterm elections, as the House struggles through an often chaotic session, Greene’s announced retirement will ripple throughout the ranks – and raise questions about her next moves.

Greene was first elected to the House in 2020. She initially planned to run in a competitive district in northern Atlanta’s suburbs, but relocated to the much more conservative 14th District in Georgia’s northwest corner.
She showed a penchant for harsh rhetoric and conspiracy theories even before her election, suggesting a 2017 mass shooting in Las Vegas was a coordinated attack to spur support for new gun restrictions. In 2018, she endorsed the idea that the US government perpetrated the September 11 attacks, and mused that a “so-called” plane had hit the Pentagon.
Greene argued in 2019 that Representatives Ilhan Omar, Democrat-Minnesota, and Rashida Tlaib, Democrat-Michigan, both Muslim women, were not “official” members of Congress because they used Korans rather than Bibles in their swearing-in ceremonies.
Greene was once a sympathiser with QAnon, an online network that believes a global cabal of Satan-worshipping cannibals, including US government leaders, operates a child sex trafficking ring. She eventually distanced herself, saying she got “sucked into some of the things I had seen on the internet”.

data.title: Hong Kong talent scheme: They came for jobs. Will they stay to buy homes?
data.description:
In a spacious three-storey village house in Sai Kung on a recent sunny Saturday, Chen Wei and his family were unpacking the final boxes after their move from Shenyang, the capital of China’s northeast Liaoning province. Having come with his wife and two sons under Hong Kong’s Top Talent Pass Scheme, Chen, an e-commerce expert, represents a new force recalibrating the city’s future.
“The job opportunity was the first draw, but the final decision was for them,” Chen said, gesturing to his two 10-year-old boys, who were busy arranging schoolbooks from their new international academy. “Knowing how stressful the path can be for schooling on the mainland, the education ecosystem here is a better option.”
The family’s rented home is about a 10-minute drive away from the school. “Living near the right school was our immediate priority, and it takes some time to settle before considering buying a house,” Chen said, adding that the family would definitely be putting down roots in the city.
“Coming all the way here, we are committed to staying here for the longer term.”
Chen is part of a wave of nearly 90,000 professionals who have entered Hong Kong – in many cases with their families – under the government’s three-year-old initiative to attract talent.
The influx has driven rents to record highs, raising hopes that the scheme will also establish a new segment of homebuyers to sustain the housing market over the next decade. The challenge ahead is how to encourage newcomers to transition from renting to owning homes.
“The dynamics of Hong Kong’s property market have fundamentally shifted from local demand – which is decreasing due to falling birth rates – to a predominance of external demand,” said Ryan Ip Man-ki, vice-president of the Our Hong Kong Foundation and executive director of the Public Policy Institute. “It’s only a matter of time before mainland buyers surpass 50 per cent of total property transactions, with incoming talent being a key driver.”
The Top Talent Pass Scheme, launched in December 2022, was the government’s solution to address a brain drain during the pandemic and head off the negative effects of the city’s ageing population.
According to official figures, the government received some 140,000 applications, with around 110,000 approved as of July.
A two-year visa is granted to eligible applicants under three categories, which cover individuals with annual incomes of at least HK$2.5 million (US$321,000). The programme is also open to graduates from 199 select universities and institutions worldwide who have been working for three of the past five years.
The influx of new residents has driven up housing demand, as reflected in the surging residential rental index. The gauge rose 0.2 per cent to 200 in September – the 10th straight monthly increase – to come up just a whisker short of the historical high of 200.1 in August 2019, according to the Rating and Valuation Department.


Between January 2023 and October of this year, rents citywide increased by a cumulative 15 per cent, according to Midland Realty. Among major housing estates, the highest increase was at South Horizons in Ap Lei Chau, where rent per square foot surged 24 per cent to HK$39.60 during the period, Midland data showed. The next biggest increment was Whampoa Garden in Hung Hom, where the average rent rose 23.3 per cent to HK$40.20 per square foot.
However, home prices dropped almost 10 per cent during the same period, according to the agency’s property price index, as developers sold off new-home inventory at a discount while relatively high interest rates continued to deter many buyers.
Benny Sham, an analyst at Midland, said renters would gradually turn to buyers.
“Rising rents and falling property prices have dynamically boosted rental returns, which has prompted some renters to consider purchasing property, and investment appetites are gradually coming back,” he said.
Mainland Chinese buyers have accounted for a higher proportion of total home sales in recent years.
Thanks to Hong Kong’s scrapping of all property cooling measures in February 2024, including various stamp duties, these buyers accounted for 40 per cent of the first-hand sales and 17 per cent of secondary transactions last year, a high since tracking began in 2010, according to Midland’s data.
“Currently, mainland Chinese buyers account for about 30 to 40 per cent of the overall market transaction value,” said Mark Leung, UBS property analyst. “With more mainland professionals and students arriving in Hong Kong, the market share will continue to surge.”
Meanwhile, Hong Kong’s demographic shifts will also alter housing demand. The population is ageing, and at the same time, the fertility rate has dropped to the second-lowest level in the world.
The result is that about 120,000 people turn 65 each year, while only half that number turn 22. According to official forecasts, by 2046, the population aged 65 and above will surge to 36 per cent from 24 per cent currently.
“The Hong Kong property market’s source of demand has thus changed fundamentally, shifting from local demand, which will inevitably diminish due to declining birth rates, to external demand dominating the market,” said Our Hong Kong Foundation’s Ip.


But will talent-scheme entrants stick around to buy homes?
In August, the Immigration Department received 9,699 applications for extensions of talent-scheme visas. This included 7,394 people whose visas expired on July 31 – representing 54 per cent of the total expiring visas. In addition, 2,305 people applied for extensions before their visas were set to expire.
The department approved 8,511 of the applications, of which 95 per cent were from mainland China.
Two-thirds of those who renewed said they spent most of their time in Hong Kong, with 70 per cent renting accommodation and 13 per cent owning their own housing, according to a separate survey by the government. The remainder lived in company-provided quarters or hotels.
The numbers were encouraging, Ip said.
“Even with a 50 per cent renewal rate, it still means that the net input of talent is a lot higher than past levels,” he said, adding that from 2016 to 2019, incoming professionals numbered around 60,000 a year, and not all renewed. “Plus we are continuing to expand the quota for non-local students.”
The difficulty of moving money from mainland China to Hong Kong remained a “hindrance” to imported professionals buying homes, Ip said.
In the government’s latest business report in July, Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po acknowledged the issue.
“The Hong Kong government is actively working with relevant central government departments to explore whether we can relax these restrictions within an appropriate range,” he said. “This would make it easier for mainland buyers to transfer funds to Hong Kong without causing capital outflow.”
The continuous slide in China’s property market remains a barrier to new residents from the mainland buying homes in Hong Kong.
It took at least six months for some newcomers to cash out their properties back in mainland China, agents said, and even longer for properties in second and third-tier cities – unless they sold them at a loss.
Among the talent-scheme newcomers renewing a visa is Justin Law, a 38-year-old education consultant who came to Hong Kong in October 2023.

Law, who rents a flat in Causeway Bay, said buying a home could be on his wish list eventually.
“It’s too early to consider until I become a permanent resident, living here for seven years, or start my own family with kids,” he said. “Otherwise, there are still many uncertainties.”
Immigrants with families showed a stronger intention to stay because they were “more fully embedded in society” than professionals who were focused mostly on their careers, said Eric Fong, director of a research hub on population studies at the University of Hong Kong.
According to government figures, nearly half of those entering under the talent scheme applied for dependent visas, bringing an average of 1.7 children to Hong Kong.
For those families, like the Chens, education is often the primary anchor.
Hong Kong has been ramping up efforts to attract more international students following the launch of the “Study in Hong Kong” initiative unveiled by Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu in his policy address last year.
In this year’s policy address, Lee followed up with an announcement of a trial scheme to allow primary and secondary schools under the Direct Subsidy Scheme to expand their intake of non-local pupils on student visas who are self-financing their studies.
In 2024-25, 17,161 non-local students attended Hong Kong’s eight publicly funded universities, with 12,386 of them, or 72 per cent, from mainland China.
Law, who advises mainland Chinese parents on education in Hong Kong, said his clients increasingly saw the city as a viable option amid geopolitical tensions that reduced the appeal of countries like the US, UK and Australia.
“Properties are essential demands for family-oriented relocations, as they are staying long term for educational purposes,” Law said. “They might rent temporarily, but after the primary applicant’s first visa renewal, they will definitely consider buying a property in Hong Kong.”
HKU’s Fong said the rental market was hot now because some people saw Hong Kong as a stepping stone to their next destination. But among those who ended up calling Hong Kong home, many would underline the decision by leaving the rental market behind, he added.
“Although immigrants may not have sufficient funds to purchase property upon arrival, over time they will buy,” he said. “It isn’t merely about having a place to live. It’s a crucial symbolic step signifying their decision to stay and settle.”

data.title: ‘Limited alternatives’: why Japan will struggle to replace China’s tourists
data.description:
Japan will find it hard to replace the revenue lost if Chinese tourists keep avoiding the country amid a diplomatic row over Taiwan, a subsidiary of the data provider Fitch Solutions warned on Friday.
A sustained drop-off in visitor numbers from China would “significantly impact” Japan’s retail and tourism industries, as Chinese tourists were not only Japan’s largest source of overseas visitors but were also higher-than-average spenders, subsidiary BMI said in a research note.
In the short term, Japan’s tourism industry has “limited alternatives” and “will find it difficult to fill the gap left by Chinese tourists”, BMI said, adding that “airport duty-free shops and large department stores will suffer direct impacts”.
Tensions between China and Japan have escalated since early November, when Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi suggested that Tokyo could deploy military forces in the event of a conflict in the Taiwan Strait.
Beijing has since issued several travel warnings urging Chinese tourists to avoid Japan, while several Chinese airlines have offered full refunds for any Japan-bound flights until the end of the year.
By Monday, China’s airlines had received 491,000 cancellations on flights to Japan, according to an aviation industry analyst. A steep drop in Chinese tourists could cost the Japanese economy about 1.49 trillion yen (US$9.59 billion) over the next year, an economist at the Tokyo-based Nomura Research Institute estimated.


Chinese travellers in Japan spent an average of US$1,622 per person during the third quarter of 2025, higher than the average per capita spend of US$1,488 for international tourists visiting the country, according to BMI.
In the medium to long term, Japan might be able to partly offset any decline in Chinese tourism thanks to “incremental demand” from the United States, Australia, South Korea, Taiwan and Southeast Asia, BMI said.
“But a full recovery of the high-value retail structure driven by Chinese tourists is unlikely”, it added.
Travel industry sources, including a hotel operator in Japan, told the Post it was still too early to gauge the impact of the spat on airlines, booking agencies and accommodation providers.
Airlines would likely take a few weeks to adjust seating capacity to accommodate any lapse in bookings on China-Japan routes, said Mayur Patel, Asia head at the travel intelligence firm OAG Aviation.
He expected that by Christmas, Japan would have largely offset any temporary tourism losses through increased bookings from third countries and Chinese travellers who were undeterred by the political tensions.
Searches by Chinese travellers for trips to Singapore had risen about 15 per cent by November 18, compared with the previous week, said Subramania Bhatt, CEO of the travel marketing and technology firm China Trading Desk.
Singapore rated highly for family travel in China due to its proximity, safety and widespread use of the Chinese language, according to Bhatt. Trips to South Korea and tours of Malaysia, Singapore and Thailand were also popular with Chinese travellers, he added.

data.title: Hong Kong baseball coach ‘loving’ dedicated players, sets new Asian Games target
data.description:
Veteran baseball head coach Sean Au Hok-leung has said Hong Kong’s players showed they could eventually snap at the heels of the traditional Asian powerhouses during a promising National Games campaign.
On their way to an eighth-place finish in Zhongshan, the city team beat Sichuan for a first-ever Games victory and lost by only one run to Fujian and Shandong.
Au’s squad was largely staffed by emerging talent, with only eight over-23s permitted and just five of those allowed on the field in each match.
“We had prepared for several years, and I told the players to show their talent to the whole of China, and that Hong Kong can have a good baseball team,” said Au, whose collection of part-timers were battling with professionals on the mainland.
“We were very happy with the young players … in three to five years, we have the potential to be doing well on the international stage.”
Au said it would be impossible in that time frame to attain the levels of world No 1 side Japan, Chinese Taipei (2), South Korea (4) and China (17), but he aimed to “be the top team in Asia outside those four”.
Hong Kong are world ranked No 29, five spots beneath the Philippines.

“I’m optimistic we can do that in one to two years,” said Au, who has set his side a fifth-place goal for next year’s Asian Games. They finished sixth in 2023.
The city’s elite training group this year, spanning the men’s and women’s teams, U18s, U15s and U12s, had more than 200 players.
While hugely encouraged by the increasing depth of talent and players for local tournaments doubling over the past decade, the coach said Hong Kong were hamstrung by a paucity of facilities.
“One good field [Sai Tso Wan Recreation Ground in Lam Tin] is not enough for such a big group,” Au said. “We also have no indoor facilities, which is a problem in the summer heat and when we have bad weather.”
Hong Kong routinely train and play matches in Zhongshan, Shenzhen and Guangzhou.
Praising the government’s Leisure and Cultural Services Department for providing travel and meal subsidies, Au said: “We have to keep facing strong teams to improve.
“Three of the world’s top four teams are Asian, which shows the sport is suitable for Asians. It’s not like basketball, for example, where you rely on height. We have the abilities to perform.”
After arriving back in Hong Kong on Friday, Au’s players will have only two days off before they resume training.
Most will play in next month’s Hong Kong International Baseball Open, at Sai Tso Wan, which is set to feature three local sides, two from the mainland, one from Australia and one from Chinese Taipei.
“We have improved in every aspect on the field, and in our team spirit, since the last National Games [in 2021],” Au said.
“After the tight matches we lost, nobody was happy, but they knew they were playing well and had to keep trying their best to make history for Hong Kong baseball.
“But there is still room to improve.”
Au began playing baseball more than 40 years ago. He started learning to coach at 12 and was taking a team by 16. Now 51, he has been on the city squad staff for more than 30 years and head coach for more than 10.
“I’m loving the players we have now,” Au said. “They come after school or work for training, it’s hard for them. It’s not for the money.
“When we go abroad for training and tournaments, they have to use annual leave.
“The president [Dave Ho] and chairman [Stanley Chong Ki-ming] are making a big effort to increase baseball’s profile. I really appreciate the players and the association, and I’m optimistic for the future.”

data.title: Not all genetic questions about race are racist
data.description:
James Watson was probably racist and sexist, but possibly no worse than many people. He was just less inhibited in saying such things out loud. Such views got him into serious trouble late in his life. He was cancelled before “cancel culture” was a thing.
But in criticising Watson – a towering figure in 20th-century science who died early this month aged 97 – you need first to understand two things: the evolution – pun intended – of biology from its inherent Western racism to its more recent “woke” or politically correct status; and the distinction between the undoubtedly racist statements Watson made and his legitimate questions about human evolution and the possibility of innate racial attributes.
The latter set of questions may be taboo in some cultural and scientific circles and are certainly uncomfortable to raise in polite society, but that doesn’t make them illegitimate to ask, as Watson did.
Let’s address the forgotten history. “Two theories of racial difference predominated in Western science in the century before Darwin; neither was egalitarian,” wrote author Louis Menand in The Metaphysical Club.
Both doctrines were deeply racist. Those who advocated monogenism believed in a common human origin, but races emerged over time through different rates of degeneration. Polygenism stated that different groups of people were created separately and had different attributes and aptitudes from the start.
Well into the 19th century, some slave owners in the American South believed in polygenism for obvious reasons, though it contradicted the story of Genesis in the Bible. Both doctrines were closely associated with the 19th century “science” of crania, which claimed to discern racial differences by studying people’s head sizes and shapes.

Then came Charles Darwin. His initial supporters complained how society resisted his theory of evolution, but the reality was that many people on both sides of the Atlantic quickly embraced it.
It quickly gave birth to both social Darwinism, which justified Victorian cutthroat industrial capitalism and eugenics, then a highly respectable and progressive “science” and social practice promoted by, among others, Darwin’s half-cousin, the polymath Francis Galton.
But if evolutionary theory influenced dubious social theories, Darwin and Alfred Wallace, co-discoverer of the theory, were themselves heavily influenced by Thomas Malthus’ theory of human population and its collapse because unchecked population growth would always outrun food supply.
While Malthus wrote about the rise and fall – or the quantity – of human population, Galton tried to explain and improve its “quality”. But for Hitler and the Nazis, eugenics might have remained “respectable” in the West. Of course, it would eventually be widely discredited.

“Nazi Germany’s racial policies horrified serious geneticists, who – largely because of racists’ enthusiasm for eugenics – had begun … to put some distance between themselves and policies of human genetic selection and breeding…”, wrote historian Eric Hobsbawm in The Age of Extremes: A History of the World, 1914-1991.
Watson and Francis Crick’s discovery of the double-helical structure of DNA would support a part of evolutionary theory often referred to as the “modern synthesis” of modern biology.
But the new science could not escape controversy by, inadvertently or not, recasting the old racial debate in modern terms. Watson was a participant but not the originator of the heated rows over race and nature vs nurture.
Again Hobsbawm: “Never since the end of theological hegemony had such issues been seriously raised. Not surprisingly, they emerged from that part of the natural sciences which had always had, or seemed to have, direct implications for human affairs: genetics and evolutionary biology…

“Both these are notoriously touchy subjects, both because scientific models are themselves frequently ideological in such fields – we remember Darwin’s debt to Malthus and because they frequently feed back into politics (‘social Darwinism’). The concept of ‘race’ illustrates this interplay.”
Today, those like Watson who try to comment on the social or human implications of the life sciences are playing with fire.
“The memory of Nazi racial policies made it virtually unthinkable for liberal intellectuals (which included most scientists) to operate with this concept,” Hobsbawm wrote.
“Indeed, many doubt that it was legitimate even to enquire systematically into the genetically determined differences between human groups, for fear that the results might provide encouragement for racist opinions.”
Just as some racial groups are more susceptible to certain genetic diseases, Watson speculated that some differences in terms of cognitive ability among racial groups may have to do with genes – although such differences are not backed up by contemporary science. Nonetheless, asking about it is not necessarily racist.
